name: Lennie Tristano
abstract: Leonard Joseph Tristano (March 19, 1919 – November 18, 1978) was an American jazz pianist, composer, arranger, and teacher of jazz improvisation. Tristano studied for bachelor's and master's degrees in music in Chicago before moving to New York City in 1946.

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic
Midnight Performance: Charlie Parker Memorial Concert

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Liebman
about_quotes.evidence: Eventually I studied with him... the main thing that I got, though... I don't think there's any one thing I got, except understanding more about Lee Konitz and Warne Marsh... But it was that there's a system that you can learn this music... There was a guy who really knew what he was doing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Liebman — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sheila Jordan
about_quotes.evidence: I was studying with Lennie... Lennie was a great teacher. He just taught me to believe in myself. That‟s the most important lesson I got from Lennie Tristano.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheila Jordan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: Hal started to take lessons with uh Lennie Tristano... I took about 6 or 7 lessons and I realized that I had a lot to learn... But I took his lessons to heart
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bud Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: I went to study with him and he heard it immediately... he snapped me out of it and it was very interesting. I stayed with him for a very short period.

about_quotes.speaker: DeFranco Buddy
about_quotes.evidence: He was a manager; keep your nose out of my music... Lenny got him to put up a lot of money in my band.
about_quotes.doc_title: DeFranco Buddy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Rudy Van Gelder
about_quotes.evidence: Lennie called me and asked me to come over and help him record.
about_quotes.doc_title: Rudy Van Gelder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
